_meta.disclaimer: Testing procedures are constructed for educational purposes. Customise for your engagement. Not legal or regulatory advice.
_meta.purpose: Assessor work program for SC-GL/6-2023 technology risk management control review

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that the entity has established a board-approved technology risk governance framework with clear roles, committee structures, and reporting lines aligned with SC-GL/6-2023.

testSteps.action: Obtain the current board-approved technology risk governance framework document and verify board approval date, version history, and annual review cycle.
Review the technology risk committee charter, membership list, and meeting minutes from the last 4 quarters to confirm cross-functional representation and regular cadence.
Interview the committee chair or CISO to understand how the three lines of defence model is implemented for technology risk and how roles are delineated.
Inspect the documented technology risk appetite statement and verify alignment with the entity's business strategy and SC expectations for capital market intermediaries.
Trace reporting lines from operational technology risk management through second and third lines to verify independence and escalation paths to the board.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: All governance documents current version; committee minutes from last 4 quarters
keyQuestions: How does the board satisfy itself that the technology risk governance framework remains fit for purpose given evolving threats?
What triggers an out-of-cycle review of the governance framework?
How are technology risk governance responsibilities cascaded to business unit heads?
How does the three lines of defence model prevent conflicts of interest in technology risk decisions?
redFlags: No formal board approval or approval date older than 18 months
Technology risk committee has not met in the last 2 quarters
Three lines of defence model not documented or first/second line roles conflated

maturityIndicators.basic: Ad-hoc governance with no formal framework; technology risk decisions made informally without committee oversight
maturityIndicators.mature: Documented framework with committee structure, defined risk appetite, and quarterly reporting to the board
maturityIndicators.advanced: Integrated governance with real-time risk dashboards, dynamic risk appetite calibration, and automated escalation triggers

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that a qualified CISO or equivalent is designated with sufficient authority, independence from IT operations, and direct reporting to senior management or the board.

testSteps.action: Obtain the CISO's job description, appointment letter or board resolution confirming designation, and verify qualifications and experience relevant to capital market technology risk.
Review the organisational chart to confirm the CISO's reporting line is independent from the CTO or Head of IT Operations and has direct access to senior management or board committee.
Interview the CISO to assess their scope of authority, resource allocation, budget autonomy, and ability to escalate issues directly to the board.
Review evidence of CISO reporting to board or board committee over the last 4 quarters, including board minutes documenting discussion of CISO presentations.
Verify that the CISO has sufficient staff and budget by reviewing the security team structure and approved budget allocation for the current financial year.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Current CISO appointment documentation; board minutes from last 4 quarters; current organisational chart
keyQuestions: Does the CISO have the authority to halt a technology deployment if security risks are unacceptable?
How is the CISO's independence from technology operations ensured in practice?
What happens when the CISO's recommendations conflict with business or technology delivery priorities?
Is the CISO position filled on a full-time dedicated basis or combined with other roles?
redFlags: CISO reports to the CTO or Head of IT with no independent escalation path
Security responsibilities assigned to IT manager without dedicated CISO role
No evidence of CISO reporting to the board in the last 2 quarters

maturityIndicators.basic: Security responsibilities assigned to IT manager with no dedicated CISO; no board reporting
maturityIndicators.mature: Dedicated CISO with board reporting, defined authority, and adequate security team resources
maturityIndicators.advanced: CISO with enterprise-wide authority, strategic influence on business decisions, and seat at senior leadership table

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that a comprehensive suite of technology risk policies exists covering all key domains, is board-approved, annually reviewed, and communicated to all relevant personnel.

testSteps.action: Obtain the complete policy inventory and map it against SC-GL/6-2023 domain requirements (cybersecurity, data management, BCP, cloud, outsourcing, incident management) to identify coverage gaps.
For each policy, verify board approval evidence (signature, board resolution, or minutes recording approval) and confirm the last review date is within 12 months.
Review a sample of 3-5 policies for explicit references to SC-GL/6-2023 requirements and alignment with the entity's technology risk governance framework.
Inspect evidence of policy communication to all relevant personnel, such as training records, acknowledgement logs, or intranet publication records.
Interview 2-3 staff from different functions (e.g., trading, operations, IT) to verify awareness and understanding of key technology risk policies.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Complete policy inventory; detailed review of 3-5 policies; staff interviews across 2-3 functions
keyQuestions: How does the entity ensure policies remain current with evolving SC guidance and threat landscape?
What is the process for identifying when a new policy is needed (e.g., new technology adoption)?
How are policy exceptions managed and approved?
How does the entity verify that outsourced service providers comply with its technology risk policies?
redFlags: Key domains (e.g., cloud, incident management) have no corresponding policy
Policies not reviewed or approved by the board within the last 18 months
Staff are unaware of or unable to locate key technology risk policies

maturityIndicators.basic: Partial policy coverage with inconsistent review; some domains not addressed
maturityIndicators.mature: Complete policy suite with annual board review, SC-GL/6-2023 references, and communication records
maturityIndicators.advanced: Dynamic policies with automated compliance monitoring, version control, and real-time exception tracking

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that regular, structured technology risk reports are produced for the board and senior management, covering risk posture, KRIs, incident trends, compliance status, and emerging risks.

testSteps.action: Obtain technology risk reports submitted to the board or board committee for the last 4 quarters and verify consistent reporting cadence.
Review report content for inclusion of key risk indicators, incident trends, compliance metrics, audit remediation status, and emerging technology risk commentary.
Review board minutes to confirm discussion of technology risk reports, board questions raised, and actions or decisions taken in response.
Interview the CISO or CRO to understand the process for developing reports, data sources used, and how emerging risks are identified and included.
Verify that KRI definitions, thresholds, and escalation triggers are documented and that threshold breaches are reported and actioned.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: All board reports from last 4 quarters; corresponding board minutes; KRI definitions document
keyQuestions: How are KRI thresholds calibrated and how often are they reviewed?
What emerging technology risks have been reported to the board in the last 12 months?
How does the board use risk reports to inform strategic technology decisions?
How is the remediation status of audit findings reported and tracked?
redFlags: Reports produced ad-hoc or only after incidents rather than on a quarterly cadence
Reports limited to incident counts without risk context, KRIs, or trend analysis
No evidence of board discussion or actions arising from technology risk reports

maturityIndicators.basic: Ad-hoc reporting on incidents only; no KRIs defined; no regular board reporting cadence
maturityIndicators.mature: Structured quarterly reporting with defined KRIs, trend analysis, and documented board discussion
maturityIndicators.advanced: Real-time risk dashboard with predictive analytics, automated threshold alerting, and integrated remediation tracking

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that the entity has a structured approach to identifying, assessing, and responding to cyber threats targeting capital market systems, including threat intelligence and threat hunting.

testSteps.action: Obtain documentation of the threat intelligence programme, including subscribed feeds (government, industry, commercial), and verify feeds are active and relevant to capital market threats.
Review the last 2 periodic threat assessment reports to verify coverage of capital market-specific threats (e.g., trading system attacks, market manipulation, DDoS targeting market infrastructure).
Interview SOC analysts or threat intelligence staff to understand threat hunting activities, tools used, and how threat intelligence is operationalised into detection rules.
Review evidence of participation in SC or industry threat information sharing initiatives (e.g., MyCERT, SC advisories, industry ISACs or working groups).
Inspect a sample of 3-5 threat intelligence items that were received, assessed, and actioned (e.g., new IOCs deployed, detection rules updated, patches prioritised) to verify the end-to-end process.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Last 2 threat assessment reports; sample 3-5 actioned threat intelligence items; participation records for last 12 months
keyQuestions: How does the entity prioritise threat intelligence relevant to its capital market operations?
What threat hunting activities have been conducted in the last 12 months and what were the outcomes?
How quickly are new indicators of compromise deployed to detection systems?
What channels are used for threat information sharing with the SC and peer institutions?
redFlags: No subscribed threat intelligence feeds or reliance on publicly available information only
Threat assessments do not address capital market-specific threat scenarios
No evidence of threat hunting or proactive detection activities

maturityIndicators.basic: Reactive threat response only; no threat intelligence programme; no industry participation
maturityIndicators.mature: Proactive threat intelligence with SOC integration, periodic threat assessments, and industry sharing
maturityIndicators.advanced: Advanced threat hunting with ML-based detection, automated IOC deployment, and real-time cross-sector sharing

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that security monitoring capabilities provide continuous surveillance of capital market systems with adequate coverage, detection rules, and response metrics.

testSteps.action: Review the SIEM deployment documentation, log source inventory, and confirm that all critical trading, settlement, and market data systems are feeding logs into the SIEM.
Verify 24/7 monitoring coverage for critical systems by reviewing SOC shift rosters, staffing model (in-house or MSSP), and after-hours escalation procedures.

Inspect a sample of 5-10 SIEM detection rules and assess whether they are tuned for capital market-specific attack patterns (e.g., unusual trading activity, market data manipulation, privileged access abuse).
Review SOC performance metrics for the last 2 quarters, including mean time to detect (MTTD), mean time to respond (MTTR), false positive rates, and alert volumes.
Obtain the most recent SOC effectiveness assessment or maturity review and verify that recommendations have been tracked and addressed.
Observe the SOC environment (if on-site) to assess analyst tools, runbook availability, and escalation mechanisms in practice.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Log source inventory (full); sample 5-10 detection rules; SOC metrics for last 2 quarters; latest effectiveness assessment
keyQuestions: What percentage of critical capital market systems are covered by SOC monitoring?
How are detection rules reviewed and updated in response to new threats?
What is the current MTTD and MTTR for critical severity alerts?
How does the SOC differentiate between trading anomalies and security incidents?
redFlags: Critical trading or settlement systems not included in SIEM log collection
Monitoring limited to business hours only for systems that operate outside trading hours
No SOC effectiveness assessment conducted in the last 2 years

maturityIndicators.basic: Basic log monitoring during business hours; limited detection rules; no performance metrics
maturityIndicators.mature: 24/7 SOC with SIEM and SOAR, market-specific detection rules, and tracked MTTD/MTTR metrics
maturityIndicators.advanced: AI-augmented SOC with automated response orchestration, continuous detection engineering, and proactive threat hunting

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that the entity conducts systematic vulnerability identification, assessment, and remediation for all capital market technology systems with defined SLAs.

testSteps.action: Obtain the vulnerability management policy or procedure and verify it defines scanning frequency, asset scope, risk-based prioritisation criteria, and remediation SLAs by severity.
Review 5 recent vulnerability scan reports to verify scope includes all critical capital market systems (trading, settlement, client portals) and that scan frequency meets policy requirements.
Inspect the vulnerability remediation tracker and select a sample of 10 critical or high findings to verify they were remediated within the defined SLA.
Obtain the most recent penetration testing report and verify it was conducted by qualified independent testers with scope covering critical market systems.
Review the exception or risk acceptance register for unpatched vulnerabilities to verify appropriate approval and compensating controls are in place.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Sample 5 vulnerability scan reports; sample 10 critical/high findings for SLA compliance; latest penetration test report
keyQuestions: What are the defined SLAs for patching critical, high, medium, and low vulnerabilities?
How are emergency patches (zero-day) handled for production trading systems?
What percentage of vulnerabilities are currently overdue for remediation?
Are trading systems included in or excluded from automated patching, and how is this risk managed?
redFlags: Vulnerability scanning excludes trading or settlement systems from scope
Critical vulnerabilities remain unpatched beyond SLA with no documented risk acceptance
No penetration testing conducted in the last 12 months

maturityIndicators.basic: Ad-hoc patching with no regular scanning; patching driven by vendor bulletins only
maturityIndicators.mature: Regular scanning with risk-based patching SLAs, tracked remediation, and annual penetration testing
maturityIndicators.advanced: Continuous scanning with automated patch deployment, real-time vulnerability dashboards, and predictive risk scoring

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that the network architecture provides appropriate segmentation, access controls, and protection for trading, settlement, and market data networks.

testSteps.action: Obtain the current network architecture diagram and verify segmentation between trading networks, settlement systems, corporate networks, and DMZ/internet-facing zones.
Review firewall rule sets for inter-segment traffic to verify that access between zones follows least-privilege principles, particularly between corporate and trading networks.
Inspect IDS/IPS deployment and verify coverage of market infrastructure segments, including review of a sample of 5 recent IDS/IPS alerts and response actions.
Review security controls for market data feeds and connectivity with external participants (e.g., Bursa Malaysia, clearing houses) including encryption and authentication mechanisms.
Obtain the most recent network architecture review report and verify it assessed the architecture against the evolving threat landscape and identified remediation actions.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Current network diagram; firewall rule sets for key inter-segment paths; sample 5 IDS/IPS alerts; latest architecture review
keyQuestions: How is the trading network isolated from the corporate network and the internet?
What controls protect market data feed connectivity with Bursa Malaysia and other participants?
When was the last network architecture review conducted and what changes resulted?
How are remote access connections to trading infrastructure secured?
redFlags: Flat network with no segmentation between trading and corporate environments
Firewall rules include overly permissive 'any-any' rules between zones
No IDS/IPS deployed on segments hosting critical market infrastructure

maturityIndicators.basic: Flat network with basic perimeter firewall; no segmentation between trading and corporate
maturityIndicators.mature: Segmented network with IDS/IPS, monitored inter-segment traffic, and annual architecture review
maturityIndicators.advanced: Zero-trust architecture with microsegmentation, software-defined perimeters, and continuous posture assessment

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that a systematic technology risk assessment framework exists, annual assessments are conducted, and results are integrated with enterprise risk management.

testSteps.action: Obtain the technology risk assessment methodology document and verify it defines risk identification, likelihood and impact assessment criteria, risk rating scales, and treatment options aligned with SC-GL/6-2023.
Review the most recent annual technology risk assessment report and verify it covers all capital market technology domains including trading, settlement, market data, and support systems.
Verify that algorithmic trading system risks and market infrastructure dependency risks have been specifically assessed, given their significance to capital market intermediaries.
Interview the CRO or risk management team to understand how technology risk assessment results feed into the enterprise risk register and risk appetite monitoring.
Select a sample of 5 high-rated technology risks and trace them through to treatment plans, owners, and implementation status to verify end-to-end risk treatment.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Current methodology document; latest annual assessment report; sample 5 high-rated risks and treatment plans
keyQuestions: How does the technology risk assessment methodology align with the enterprise risk framework?
How are risks from algorithmic trading systems specifically identified and assessed?
What triggers an out-of-cycle technology risk assessment?
How are residual risks monitored after treatment implementation?
redFlags: No formal technology risk assessment conducted in the last 12 months
Assessment does not specifically address capital market-specific risks (trading, settlement, market data)
High-rated risks with no documented treatment plan or assigned owner

maturityIndicators.basic: Ad-hoc risk identification with no defined methodology or formal assessment process
maturityIndicators.mature: Structured annual assessment with defined methodology, ERM integration, and treatment tracking
maturityIndicators.advanced: Continuous risk assessment with automated risk signals, real-time risk register, and predictive risk analytics

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that pre-trade and post-trade risk controls are implemented for algorithmic trading systems, including kill switches, position limits, and market manipulation prevention.

testSteps.action: Obtain documentation of all pre-trade risk controls (price limits, order size limits, position limits) and verify they are configured in the trading system with appropriate thresholds.
Request a demonstration or evidence of the kill switch capability, including who has authority to activate it, the activation process, and records of any tests or activations.

Review the algorithm testing and approval process documentation, including evidence of backtesting, simulation testing, and formal sign-off before production deployment for the most recent 3 algorithms.
Inspect post-trade surveillance reports for the last quarter to verify monitoring for market manipulation patterns (e.g., spoofing, layering, wash trading) in algorithmic activity.
Interview the Head of Trading or risk controls team to understand how limit breaches are detected, escalated, and resolved in real-time during market hours.
Review records of any limit breaches or kill switch activations in the last 12 months and verify appropriate investigation and follow-up actions were taken.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: All pre-trade control configurations; kill switch test records; last 3 algorithm approvals; post-trade surveillance reports for last quarter
keyQuestions: What pre-trade risk checks are applied to all algorithmic orders before execution?
How quickly can the kill switch halt all algorithmic trading, and has it been tested?
How are position limit breaches detected and escalated during market hours?
What post-trade surveillance is conducted to detect potential market manipulation by algorithms?
redFlags: Kill switch capability not implemented or not tested in the last 12 months
Pre-trade limits not calibrated to current market conditions or trading volumes
No post-trade surveillance for market manipulation patterns in algorithmic activity

maturityIndicators.basic: Basic order size limits only; no kill switch; no post-trade surveillance
maturityIndicators.mature: Comprehensive pre-trade and post-trade controls with tested kill switch and manipulation monitoring
maturityIndicators.advanced: Real-time ML-based anomaly detection with automated circuit breakers and adaptive limit calibration

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that technology changes are risk-assessed before implementation with classification, testing, approval gates, and rollback procedures proportionate to change risk level.

testSteps.action: Obtain the change management policy and verify it defines change risk classification criteria (critical, significant, standard) with corresponding approval and testing requirements.
Select a sample of 5 recent changes to critical trading or settlement systems and verify each has a documented risk assessment, test results, and appropriate approval before production deployment.
For the same sample, verify that rollback procedures were defined and that post-implementation reviews were conducted.
Review the change log for the last quarter and identify any emergency changes; verify emergency changes were retrospectively risk-assessed and approved.
Interview the change manager to understand how changes to trading systems are coordinated with market operating hours and Bursa Malaysia schedules.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Change management policy; sample 5 critical/significant changes; change log for last quarter; emergency change records
keyQuestions: How are changes to trading systems scheduled relative to market operating hours?
What percentage of changes are classified as emergency and how is this monitored?
How are rollback decisions made during market hours if a change causes issues?
Who has authority to approve changes to critical market systems?
redFlags: Critical changes deployed to production without documented risk assessment or approval
No rollback procedures defined for changes to trading or settlement systems
High proportion of emergency changes (>15%) suggesting process bypass

maturityIndicators.basic: Changes deployed without risk assessment; ad-hoc testing; no formal approval gates
maturityIndicators.mature: Risk-based change classification with approval gates, defined rollback, and post-implementation review
maturityIndicators.advanced: Automated risk scoring with continuous deployment pipelines, canary releases, and automated rollback

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that cloud-specific risk assessments are conducted for all cloud adoptions, covering shared responsibility, data sovereignty, and capital market-specific considerations.

testSteps.action: Obtain the cloud risk assessment framework or methodology and verify it addresses shared responsibility, multi-tenancy, data residency, and regulatory requirements for capital market data.
Review cloud risk assessments for all current cloud engagements involving client data or market systems, verifying each was conducted before cloud adoption.
Inspect the shared responsibility matrix for the primary cloud service provider and verify it clearly delineates security responsibilities between the entity and the CSP.
Review CSP certifications and independent assurance reports (e.g., SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001) and verify they are current and cover the services used by the entity.
Verify that data residency implications for client information and market data have been assessed, and that data is stored in jurisdictions consistent with SC and PDPA requirements.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: All cloud risk assessments for engagements involving client or market data; CSP assurance reports for all primary providers
keyQuestions: Which capital market systems or data are hosted in cloud environments?
Where is client data physically stored, and how is data residency verified?
How are CSP assurance report findings assessed for relevance to the entity's risk profile?
Has the SC been notified of material cloud outsourcing arrangements as required?
redFlags: Cloud services adopted without prior risk assessment for client data or market systems
No shared responsibility matrix documented for material cloud engagements
Client data stored in jurisdictions that do not meet PDPA or SC requirements

maturityIndicators.basic: No cloud-specific risk assessment; cloud adoption decisions made without security input
maturityIndicators.mature: Structured cloud risk framework applied per engagement with shared responsibility matrices and assurance review
maturityIndicators.advanced: Continuous cloud security posture monitoring with automated compliance checks and real-time risk assessment

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that data governance controls are implemented for cloud-hosted capital market data, including classification, encryption, access controls, and data residency compliance.

testSteps.action: Obtain an inventory of data types stored in cloud environments and verify classification labels are applied consistent with the entity's data classification scheme.
Verify encryption controls for sensitive data in cloud: inspect encryption configuration for data at rest (e.g., customer-managed keys, BYOK) and data in transit.
Review access control configurations for cloud environments hosting client data, including IAM policies, multi-factor authentication, and privileged access management.
Inspect data residency configuration and monitoring evidence to confirm client information is stored in compliant jurisdictions and that residency requirements are enforced technically.
Review cloud access logs and audit trails for the last quarter to verify data access monitoring is active and anomalous access patterns are investigated.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Cloud data inventory; encryption configurations for all cloud environments with sensitive data; IAM policies; access logs for last quarter
keyQuestions: Are customer-managed encryption keys used for client and market data in the cloud?
How is data residency enforced technically, not just contractually?
Who has privileged access to cloud environments hosting client data, and how is this reviewed?
How are data access anomalies in cloud environments detected and investigated?
redFlags: Client data encrypted with CSP-managed keys only, with no customer key management
No data classification applied to cloud-hosted data assets
Privileged access to cloud environments not subject to MFA or regular review

maturityIndicators.basic: Basic encryption with CSP-managed keys; minimal access controls; no data classification in cloud
maturityIndicators.mature: BYOK encryption with data classification, RBAC, access monitoring, and data residency verification
maturityIndicators.advanced: Automated data governance with DLP, rights management, real-time classification, and continuous compliance monitoring

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that exit strategies are developed and maintained for cloud services to ensure data portability and continuity of capital market operations if CSP failure or contract termination occurs.

testSteps.action: Obtain documented exit plans for all material cloud deployments hosting capital market systems or client data, and verify they cover data extraction, migration, and service continuity.
Review cloud workload designs for portability considerations, such as use of open standards, containerisation, and avoidance of proprietary lock-in for critical services.
Verify that data extraction and migration procedures have been tested, including timing estimates and confirmation that all data can be retrieved in usable formats.
Review contractual provisions with CSPs for exit assistance, data retrieval timelines, and data deletion obligations post-termination.
Interview the CTO or cloud architecture team to understand identified alternative providers or on-premises fallback options for critical cloud services.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Exit plans for all material cloud deployments; CSP contracts for exit provisions; data migration test records
keyQuestions: For each material cloud service, what is the estimated time and cost to exit?
Have data extraction procedures been tested end-to-end?
What alternative providers or on-premises options have been identified for critical cloud services?
Do CSP contracts include adequate exit assistance and data deletion obligations?
redFlags: No documented exit strategy for material cloud deployments hosting client or market data
Data extraction procedures never tested; estimated exit timelines unknown
Heavy reliance on proprietary CSP services with no portability assessment

maturityIndicators.basic: No cloud exit planning; no portability considerations; vendor lock-in not assessed
maturityIndicators.mature: Documented exit plans with portability requirements, tested data extraction, and identified alternatives
maturityIndicators.advanced: Tested multi-cloud failover with automated migration, open standards, and regular exit rehearsals

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that risk assessments are conducted for all third-party technology service providers, with depth proportionate to service criticality and access to client data or market systems.

testSteps.action: Obtain the complete third-party technology service provider register and verify all vendors are classified by risk tier based on service criticality and data access.
Select a sample of 5 critical-tier vendors and review the due diligence assessments conducted, verifying depth of assessment is proportionate to the risk tier.
For the same sample, verify that vendor security posture was assessed through questionnaires, independent security reviews, or assurance reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001).
Review evidence of ongoing vendor risk monitoring, including annual reassessment for critical vendors and monitoring of vendor financial health and operational stability.
Interview vendor risk management staff to understand the end-to-end process from vendor onboarding through ongoing monitoring and how risk findings trigger remediation.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Complete vendor register; detailed review of 5 critical-tier vendors; reassessment records for last 12 months
keyQuestions: How are vendors classified into risk tiers and what criteria drive the classification?
For critical vendors with access to client data, what independent assurance is obtained?
How frequently are critical vendor assessments refreshed?
What process is followed if a vendor's risk profile deteriorates?
redFlags: No vendor risk tiering; all vendors treated equally regardless of criticality
Due diligence limited to onboarding with no ongoing reassessment
Cloud service providers not included in the third-party risk assessment programme

maturityIndicators.basic: Basic vendor checks at onboarding only; no risk tiering or ongoing monitoring
maturityIndicators.mature: Tiered assessment programme with annual reviews, independent assurance for critical vendors, and ongoing monitoring
maturityIndicators.advanced: Continuous vendor risk monitoring with automated scoring, real-time alerts, and integrated risk dashboards

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that governance structures for technology outsourcing are aligned with SC-GL/6-2023, including board oversight, contractual standards, and monitoring mechanisms.

testSteps.action: Obtain the outsourcing policy and verify it is aligned with SC-GL/6-2023 requirements, including definitions of material outsourcing, board approval requirements, and governance structures.
Review board approval records for all material outsourcing arrangements and verify the board was provided with adequate information on risks, controls, and monitoring mechanisms.
Select 3 material outsourcing contracts and inspect for mandatory provisions: SLAs, right-to-audit, data protection, business continuity, termination and exit, and regulatory access.
Review quarterly outsourcing risk reports to the board and verify reporting covers performance, incidents, risk changes, and compliance with contractual obligations.
Verify that SC notification requirements for material outsourcing have been met, where applicable.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Outsourcing policy; board approval records for all material arrangements; 3 material outsourcing contracts; quarterly reports for last 4 quarters
keyQuestions: How does the entity define 'material outsourcing' and what arrangements currently meet this threshold?
Does the board receive regular reporting on outsourcing risks and vendor performance?
Do all material outsourcing contracts include the right to audit and regulatory access provisions?
Has the SC been notified of material outsourcing arrangements as required?
redFlags: Material outsourcing arrangements not approved by the board
Contracts missing mandatory provisions such as right-to-audit or data protection clauses
No regular outsourcing risk reporting to the board

maturityIndicators.basic: Informal outsourcing management; no policy; board not involved in outsourcing decisions
maturityIndicators.mature: Formal governance with policy, board oversight, standardised contracts, and quarterly reporting
maturityIndicators.advanced: Integrated outsourcing governance with enterprise risk, automated contract compliance monitoring, and real-time dashboards

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that service provider performance is monitored against agreed service levels with escalation procedures for persistent underperformance.

testSteps.action: Obtain the SLA monitoring framework and verify it defines how vendor performance is measured, reported, and escalated for all outsourced technology services.
Review monthly performance reports for 3 critical vendors from the last quarter and verify SLA metrics are tracked, deviations highlighted, and trends analysed.
Select 2-3 instances of SLA breaches and trace the escalation process to verify timely notification, root cause analysis, remediation actions, and service credits or penalties applied.
Review evidence of periodic vendor relationship reviews or governance meetings (e.g., minutes, action logs) for critical vendors.
Interview the service management team to understand how persistent underperformance is identified and what actions are taken (e.g., remediation plans, vendor replacement).

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Monthly performance reports for 3 critical vendors over last quarter; 2-3 SLA breach escalation records; vendor governance meeting records
keyQuestions: How are SLA metrics collected - through vendor self-reporting or independent monitoring?
What happens when a vendor persistently fails to meet SLAs?
How often are vendor governance meetings held for critical service providers?
Are service credits or penalties applied for SLA breaches, and have they been invoked?
redFlags: Reliance solely on vendor self-reporting for SLA compliance with no independent verification
SLA breaches not escalated or not resulting in documented remediation actions
No vendor governance meetings or performance reviews for critical service providers

maturityIndicators.basic: Reliance on vendor self-reporting; no systematic performance monitoring or escalation
maturityIndicators.mature: Automated SLA monitoring with exception reporting, escalation procedures, and regular governance reviews
maturityIndicators.advanced: Predictive performance analytics with proactive management, automated escalation, and continuous vendor scoring

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that risks from service providers' sub-contractors are identified and managed, particularly where they impact capital market operations or access client data.

testSteps.action: Obtain the fourth-party register or sub-contractor disclosure records for all critical service providers and verify completeness against contractual disclosure requirements.
Review contracts with critical vendors to verify they include provisions requiring disclosure of sub-contracting, approval requirements, and flow-down of security and data protection obligations.
Select 2 critical supply chains and assess fourth-party risk assessments conducted, including concentration risk and geographic considerations.
Review evidence of monitoring for supply chain changes, such as vendor notifications of sub-contractor changes and the entity's response and reassessment actions.
Interview vendor risk management staff to understand how fourth-party concentration risks are identified and mitigated (e.g., multiple critical vendors using the same CSP).

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Fourth-party register; contracts for 3-5 critical vendors (sub-contracting clauses); 2 critical supply chain assessments
keyQuestions: Which critical vendors use sub-contractors that access client data or market systems?
Are there concentration risks where multiple vendors depend on the same fourth party?
How are you notified when a vendor changes its sub-contractors?
Do contracts require vendor approval before introducing new sub-contractors for critical services?
redFlags: No visibility into sub-contractors used by critical service providers
Contracts do not require sub-contractor disclosure or approval
Concentration risk not assessed (e.g., multiple vendors dependent on the same cloud provider)

maturityIndicators.basic: No fourth-party visibility; sub-contractors not disclosed or tracked
maturityIndicators.mature: Fourth-party register with contractual disclosure requirements and periodic assessment
maturityIndicators.advanced: Continuous supply chain risk monitoring with automated concentration risk alerts and real-time sub-contractor tracking

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that a comprehensive data governance framework exists for capital market data, covering classification, quality management, and compliance with PDPA and SC requirements.

testSteps.action: Obtain the data governance framework document and verify it defines roles (data owners, stewards, custodians), responsibilities, accountability structures, and reporting lines.
Review the data classification scheme and inspect a sample of 5-10 data assets to verify classification labels are applied consistently across market data, client data, and operational data.
Inspect data quality standards and monitoring mechanisms, including data quality metrics, dashboards, and remediation processes for data quality issues.
Verify that PDPA compliance has been assessed and documented, including data processing notices, consent mechanisms, and data subject access request procedures for client data.
Interview the CDO or DPO to understand how data governance is enforced across the entity, including for cloud-hosted and outsourced data processing.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Data governance framework document; classification scheme applied to sample of 5-10 data assets; PDPA compliance assessment; data quality reports
keyQuestions: How are data ownership and stewardship roles assigned and enforced?
What data quality metrics are tracked and how are quality issues remediated?
How does the data governance framework address data held by outsourced service providers?
Has a PDPA compliance assessment been conducted and what were the findings?
redFlags: No formal data governance framework; data management handled ad-hoc
Data classification scheme not applied to client data or market data assets
PDPA compliance not assessed or documented for client data processing

maturityIndicators.basic: No formal data governance; data not classified; no quality monitoring
maturityIndicators.mature: Documented framework with classification, quality controls, data ownership, and PDPA compliance assessment
maturityIndicators.advanced: Automated data governance with lineage tracking, real-time quality monitoring, and continuous PDPA compliance

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that controls ensure the integrity, accuracy, and timeliness of market data used in trading, settlement, and reporting operations.

testSteps.action: Obtain documentation of data validation checks implemented on market data feeds and verify they cover completeness, accuracy, timeliness, and format integrity.
Review market data latency and accuracy metrics for the last quarter, including any breaches of defined thresholds and corrective actions taken.
Inspect reconciliation processes for critical market data, including reconciliation between internal systems and external sources (e.g., Bursa Malaysia price feeds).
Review data correction and restatement procedures and inspect records of any market data corrections applied in the last 6 months.
Interview the Head of Market Operations to understand how market data integrity issues are detected, escalated, and resolved during trading hours.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Validation rule documentation; market data metrics for last quarter; reconciliation reports; data correction records for last 6 months
keyQuestions: What validation checks are applied to market data feeds before use in trading systems?
How are market data discrepancies between internal systems and exchange feeds detected and resolved?
What is the process for correcting erroneous market data that has been used in trades or valuations?
How is market data feed latency monitored and what are the acceptable thresholds?
redFlags: No automated validation checks on market data feeds before use in trading systems
No reconciliation between internal market data and external sources
Market data corrections applied without documented approval or audit trail

maturityIndicators.basic: Manual data quality checks; no automated validation; limited reconciliation
maturityIndicators.mature: Automated validation with reconciliation, latency monitoring, and documented correction procedures
maturityIndicators.advanced: Real-time data quality monitoring with anomaly detection, automated reconciliation, and predictive integrity checks

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that data protection controls are implemented for client information, including encryption, access controls, and data loss prevention measures.

testSteps.action: Inspect encryption configurations for client data at rest across databases, file storage, and backups, verifying encryption standards meet industry benchmarks (AES-256 or equivalent).
Verify that client data in transit is encrypted using TLS 1.2 or higher for all channels including client portals, API interfaces, and internal data transfers.
Review role-based access control configurations for client information systems and verify access rights are assigned based on least privilege with periodic access reviews.
Inspect DLP tool deployment and policies, including a sample of 5 DLP alerts from the last quarter to verify monitoring effectiveness and response to potential data leakage.
Obtain the most recent data protection impact assessment and verify it covers all client data processing activities, including those performed by third parties.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Encryption configurations for all client data stores; RBAC configurations; sample 5 DLP alerts; latest DPIA
keyQuestions: What encryption standards are used for client data at rest and in transit?
How frequently are access rights to client information systems reviewed?
What DLP controls are in place to prevent unauthorised exfiltration of client data?
Has a data protection impact assessment been conducted for all client data processing activities?
redFlags: Client data stored unencrypted or using outdated encryption standards
Overly broad access to client information systems with no periodic access review
No DLP tools deployed or DLP alerts not monitored and actioned

maturityIndicators.basic: Basic encryption with minimal access controls; no DLP; access reviews not conducted
maturityIndicators.mature: Comprehensive DLP with RBAC, encryption, regular access reviews, and data protection impact assessments
maturityIndicators.advanced: Zero-trust data protection with automated classification, adaptive access controls, and real-time DLP orchestration

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that a business continuity management programme exists specific to capital market operations, with BIA, defined RTOs/RPOs, and coordination with market operators.

testSteps.action: Obtain the business impact analysis for capital market functions and verify it covers trading, clearing, settlement, custody, and client services with assigned criticality levels.
Review the BCP document and verify it includes recovery strategies for trading, clearing, settlement, and custody operations with defined RTO and RPO for each.
Verify that RTO and RPO targets have been validated through testing, and that current recovery capabilities can meet the defined targets.
Review evidence of coordination with Bursa Malaysia, clearing houses, and other market infrastructure providers for business continuity planning.
Interview the BCM Manager or COO to understand how the BCP is maintained, the frequency of reviews, and how lessons from disruptions or tests are incorporated.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: BIA covering all capital market functions; BCP document; RTO/RPO validation evidence; coordination records with market operators
keyQuestions: What are the defined RTOs and RPOs for trading, clearing, and settlement systems?
How does the entity coordinate business continuity with Bursa Malaysia and clearing houses?
When was the BIA last updated and what changes resulted?
What alternative operating procedures are in place for prolonged system outages during market hours?
redFlags: No BIA conducted or BIA does not cover critical capital market functions
RTOs and RPOs not defined or not validated through testing
No coordination with market infrastructure providers for business continuity

maturityIndicators.basic: Basic BCP without market-specific considerations; no BIA; RTOs/RPOs undefined
maturityIndicators.mature: Comprehensive BCP with market operation focus, validated RTOs/RPOs, and market operator coordination
maturityIndicators.advanced: Dynamic BCP with real-time impact assessment, automated recovery orchestration, and continuous readiness monitoring

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that trading systems have sufficient resilience, redundancy, and failover capabilities to maintain market access during technology disruptions.

testSteps.action: Obtain the trading system architecture documentation and verify redundancy design including active-passive or active-active configurations, data replication, and failover mechanisms.
Review capacity planning documentation and verify headroom is provisioned for market volatility scenarios (e.g., 2x or 3x normal trading volume).
Inspect records of trading system failover tests from the last 12 months, including test objectives, results, failover times, and any issues identified.
Verify that alternative trading channels or manual trading procedures are documented and tested for use when primary trading systems are unavailable.
Interview the Head of Trading Technology to understand how trading system health is monitored in real-time and what automated alerts trigger failover procedures.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Trading system architecture documentation; capacity planning documents; failover test records for last 12 months; alternative channel procedures
keyQuestions: What is the failover time for the primary trading system and has it been validated?
How much capacity headroom is provisioned for high-volatility market events?
What alternative trading channels are available if the primary system fails during market hours?
When was the last trading system failover test conducted and what was the outcome?
redFlags: Single trading system with no redundancy or failover capability
No failover test conducted in the last 12 months
No alternative trading channel documented for primary system failures

maturityIndicators.basic: Single trading system with manual recovery; no failover testing; no capacity headroom planning
maturityIndicators.mature: Redundant systems with tested failover, capacity headroom, and documented alternative trading channels
maturityIndicators.advanced: Active-active trading with zero-downtime failover, auto-scaling for volatility, and real-time health monitoring

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that regular disaster recovery testing is conducted for capital market systems, including participation in industry-wide exercises and remediation of gaps.

testSteps.action: Obtain the DR testing plan for the current year and verify it covers all critical market systems with defined test objectives, scenarios, and success criteria.
Review DR test reports from the last 12 months and verify they document test execution, results, RTO/RPO achievement, data integrity checks, and issues identified.
Verify participation in industry-wide DR exercises coordinated by SC or Bursa Malaysia, including evidence of participation and follow-up actions from exercise findings.
Review the DR gap remediation tracker and verify that issues identified in testing are tracked to closure with assigned owners and target dates.
Interview IT Operations or BCM Manager to understand how DR test scenarios are selected and whether they include realistic failure scenarios beyond basic switchover.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Current year DR test plan; all DR test reports from last 12 months; industry exercise participation records; gap remediation tracker
keyQuestions: How many DR tests were conducted in the last 12 months and what systems were covered?
Were RTOs and RPOs achieved during DR testing, and if not, what remediation was taken?
Did the entity participate in the latest SC or Bursa-coordinated industry DR exercise?
What realistic failure scenarios are included in DR testing beyond basic switchover?
redFlags: No DR testing conducted in the last 12 months for critical market systems
Industry-wide DR exercises not participated in despite being invited
DR test findings not remediated or tracked to closure

maturityIndicators.basic: No regular DR testing; testing ad-hoc or reactive only
maturityIndicators.mature: Annual DR testing with industry participation, documented results, and tracked remediation
maturityIndicators.advanced: Continuous DR readiness with automated failover testing, chaos engineering, and real-time readiness metrics

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that settlement processes can continue during technology disruptions with alternative procedures and coordination with central depositories and clearing houses.

testSteps.action: Obtain documented alternative settlement procedures for use during system failures and verify they cover manual processing, communication protocols, and reconciliation steps.
Review evidence of coordination with Bursa Malaysia Depository (BMD) for settlement continuity, including agreed communication protocols and escalation procedures.
Inspect test records for manual settlement fallback procedures, including the most recent test results and any issues identified.
Review communication protocols maintained with clearing and depository participants for use during settlement disruptions.
Interview the Head of Operations or Settlement Manager to understand the process for transitioning from automated to manual settlement and the maximum volume that can be handled manually.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Alternative settlement procedures document; BMD coordination records; manual fallback test records; communication protocols
keyQuestions: What is the maximum settlement volume that can be processed manually during a system outage?
How are settlement instructions communicated to BMD when primary systems are unavailable?
When were manual settlement fallback procedures last tested?
How is reconciliation performed after reverting from manual to automated settlement?
redFlags: No alternative settlement procedures documented for system failures
Manual settlement fallback never tested or last tested more than 18 months ago
No coordination arrangements with BMD for settlement disruption scenarios

maturityIndicators.basic: No alternative settlement procedures; reliance on primary system availability only
maturityIndicators.mature: Documented manual fallback with testing, BMD coordination, and communication protocols
maturityIndicators.advanced: Automated settlement failover with real-time coordination, automatic reconciliation, and seamless recovery

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that a technology incident response plan exists specific to capital market operations, with classification, escalation, containment, and recovery procedures.

testSteps.action: Obtain the current incident response plan and verify it defines incident classification and severity levels appropriate for capital market operations (e.g., trading halt, settlement failure, data breach).
Review the incident response team roster, roles and responsibilities, and verify team members are aware of their roles and contact details are current.
Inspect escalation procedures, including SC notification triggers, board notification thresholds, and client communication protocols for material incidents.
Review records of tabletop exercises or incident simulations from the last 12 months, including scenario design, participation, findings, and improvement actions.
Select 2-3 actual incidents from the last 12 months and trace them through the incident response process to verify the plan was followed (classification, containment, resolution, closure).
Interview the Incident Manager to assess readiness, including how on-call arrangements work and how incidents during market hours are prioritised.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Current IRP; team roster; exercise records from last 12 months; 2-3 actual incident records
keyQuestions: How are incidents classified by severity and what determines escalation to senior management or the board?
How frequently are tabletop exercises conducted and what scenarios are used?
How are incidents during market trading hours prioritised and managed differently?
What triggers SC notification and what is the target notification timeline?
redFlags: No documented incident response plan or plan not updated in the last 18 months
No tabletop exercises or simulations conducted in the last 12 months
Actual incidents not managed according to the documented plan

maturityIndicators.basic: Ad-hoc incident response; no documented plan; no exercises conducted
maturityIndicators.mature: Documented plan with regular exercises, defined severity levels, and tested SC notification procedures
maturityIndicators.advanced: Automated incident orchestration with playbooks, real-time dashboards, and continuous exercise programme

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that the entity can notify the Securities Commission Malaysia of significant technology incidents in a timely manner as required by SC-GL/6-2023.

testSteps.action: Obtain the documented notification criteria and verify they define thresholds and conditions that trigger SC reporting, aligned with SC-GL/6-2023 requirements.
Review the notification workflow and verify it defines timelines, responsible persons, communication channels, and escalation if notification deadlines are at risk.
Inspect SC notification templates and verify they capture all required information (incident description, impact, containment actions, expected resolution).
Review records of any SC notifications submitted in the last 12 months, verifying timeliness against required deadlines and completeness of information provided.
Verify that SC responses or conditions arising from notifications have been tracked and addressed through to completion.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Notification criteria document; workflow documentation; templates; all SC notifications from last 12 months (if any)
keyQuestions: What types of incidents trigger mandatory SC notification?
What is the target timeline for notifying the SC after a reportable incident is identified?
Who is authorised to submit notifications to the SC?
How are follow-up actions from SC responses tracked and closed?
redFlags: No defined criteria for when SC notification is required
Notification process not tested through tabletop exercises
Evidence of late or incomplete notifications to the SC

maturityIndicators.basic: Manual notification with inconsistent timing; criteria not clearly defined
maturityIndicators.mature: Standardised notification process with defined criteria, templates, tracking, and tested workflow
maturityIndicators.advanced: Automated notification workflow with regulatory alerts, real-time tracking, and integrated compliance monitoring

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that the entity can coordinate cyber incident response with market participants, SC, and other authorities to contain and mitigate impact on capital market operations.

testSteps.action: Obtain documented communication protocols for coordinating with market participants, SC, MyCERT, and other relevant authorities during cyber incidents.
Review evidence of participation in industry incident response coordination mechanisms (e.g., SC working groups, industry CERTs, Bursa coordination exercises).
Inspect records of threat intelligence sharing with SC and peer institutions, including the timeliness and relevance of information shared.
Review post-incident review records where coordination with external parties was involved, and verify lessons learned were captured and incorporated.
Interview the CISO to assess the maturity of relationships with key coordination partners and the entity's ability to activate coordination mechanisms quickly.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Communication protocols; industry coordination participation records for last 12 months; threat sharing records; post-incident coordination reviews
keyQuestions: What coordination mechanisms are in place with the SC, MyCERT, and other market participants?
How quickly can the entity activate coordination protocols during a live cyber incident?
What threat intelligence has been shared with or received from industry partners in the last 12 months?
How are lessons from coordinated incident responses incorporated into the entity's plans?
redFlags: No documented communication protocols for coordination with regulators or market participants
No participation in industry incident response coordination mechanisms
No evidence of threat intelligence sharing with SC or peer institutions

maturityIndicators.basic: No coordination mechanisms; incident response handled in isolation
maturityIndicators.mature: Established protocols with key stakeholders, regular industry participation, and documented sharing
maturityIndicators.advanced: Real-time coordination platform with automated sharing, joint response playbooks, and cross-sector exercises

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that structured post-incident reviews are conducted to identify root causes, lessons learned, and improvement actions that are tracked to closure.

testSteps.action: Obtain the post-mortem policy or procedure and verify it defines when post-mortems are required, the timeframe for completion, required participants, and output format.
Select 3-5 significant incidents from the last 12 months and verify that post-mortem reviews were conducted within the defined timeframe for each.
Review the post-mortem reports for the selected incidents and verify they document root cause analysis, contributing factors, lessons learned, and specific improvement actions.
Inspect the improvement action tracker and verify that post-mortem actions are assigned to owners, have target dates, and are tracked to closure.
Review whether trend analysis is conducted across incidents to identify systemic issues, and verify that systemic findings have led to broader remediation initiatives.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Post-mortem procedure; 3-5 significant incident post-mortems from last 12 months; improvement action tracker
keyQuestions: What is the defined timeframe for completing a post-mortem after incident closure?
How are root causes distinguished from symptoms in post-mortem analysis?
How are improvement actions tracked to ensure they are implemented?
Is trend analysis conducted across incidents to identify systemic weaknesses?
redFlags: Post-mortems not conducted for significant incidents
Post-mortem reports identify root causes but improvement actions are not assigned or tracked
No trend analysis across incidents; same root causes recurring in multiple incidents

maturityIndicators.basic: Informal verbal debrief; no documented root cause analysis; no action tracking
maturityIndicators.mature: Structured post-mortem with documented root causes, tracked improvement actions, and trend review
maturityIndicators.advanced: Automated trend analysis with preventive recommendations, ML-based root cause identification, and continuous improvement metrics

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that a risk-based technology audit programme covers all key technology risk domains with qualified auditors and findings tracked to closure.

testSteps.action: Obtain the current technology audit plan and verify it is risk-based, covers all SC-GL/6-2023 domains, and defines audit scope, timing, and resource requirements.
Review the qualifications and experience of technology auditors (internal or external) to verify they have appropriate expertise in technology risk and capital market operations.
Review 2-3 completed technology audit reports and verify they provide meaningful findings, risk ratings, and actionable recommendations.
Verify that technology audit findings are reported to the board audit committee by reviewing audit committee minutes or presentation materials from the last 2 meetings.
Inspect the audit findings remediation tracker and select a sample of 10 findings to verify they are assigned to owners, have target dates, and are tracked to closure within agreed SLAs.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Current audit plan; auditor qualifications; 2-3 completed audit reports; audit committee minutes from last 2 meetings; sample 10 findings
keyQuestions: How is the technology audit plan risk-prioritised and how often is it refreshed?
Do the technology auditors have specific capital market technology experience?
How are overdue audit findings escalated to senior management?
What percentage of audit findings are currently open and overdue?
redFlags: Technology not adequately represented in the annual audit plan
Technology auditors lack relevant expertise (generic IT auditors without market system knowledge)
Significant audit findings overdue for remediation with no escalation to audit committee

maturityIndicators.basic: Ad-hoc technology audits; auditors lack technology expertise; findings not tracked
maturityIndicators.mature: Risk-based annual programme with qualified auditors, audit committee reporting, and tracked remediation
maturityIndicators.advanced: Continuous assurance with automated testing, real-time findings dashboards, and predictive risk targeting

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that the entity maintains readiness for SC regulatory examinations with current evidence, self-assessments, and rapid response capabilities.

testSteps.action: Verify that a standing evidence library is maintained for SC-GL/6-2023 requirements and that evidence items are current (within defined refresh periods).
Obtain the most recent self-assessment against SC-GL/6-2023 and verify it covers all guideline requirements with gap identification and remediation planning.
Review the examination response team composition and protocols, including designated contact persons, document production procedures, and interview coordination arrangements.
Verify that prior SC examination findings have been tracked and addressed, with evidence of remediation for each finding.
Interview Compliance or the CISO to assess confidence in examination readiness and understand the process for keeping evidence current between examinations.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Evidence library with currency verification; latest self-assessment; examination response protocols; prior examination finding tracker
keyQuestions: When was the last SC examination of technology risk management and what were the key findings?
How often is the self-assessment against SC-GL/6-2023 refreshed?
How quickly can the entity produce evidence for all SC-GL/6-2023 requirements if an examination is announced?
What is the status of remediation for prior examination findings?
redFlags: No self-assessment conducted against SC-GL/6-2023 in the last 12 months
Evidence library does not exist or evidence items are stale
Prior SC examination findings not fully remediated

maturityIndicators.basic: Scramble to prepare for examinations; no standing evidence; no self-assessment
maturityIndicators.mature: Standing evidence library with regular updates, periodic self-assessment, and defined response team
maturityIndicators.advanced: Continuous compliance monitoring with regulatory dashboard, automated evidence refresh, and always-ready posture

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that regular penetration testing is conducted by qualified independent testers with scope covering critical market systems and findings remediated within SLAs.

testSteps.action: Obtain the penetration testing policy or terms of reference and verify it defines testing frequency, scope requirements, tester qualification criteria, and reporting format.
Review the qualifications and independence of penetration testers engaged, including certifications (OSCP, CREST, etc.) and verification that testers are independent from the entity.
Review the most recent penetration test report and verify scope covers critical market systems (trading platforms, client portals, market connectivity) and client-facing applications.
Inspect the findings remediation tracker and verify critical and high findings are remediated within defined SLAs, with re-testing evidence confirming resolution.
Verify that penetration testing results are reported to the board audit committee, including trend analysis compared to prior years.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Penetration testing policy; tester qualifications; latest penetration test report; findings remediation tracker; audit committee reporting
keyQuestions: What is the scope of annual penetration testing - does it include trading systems, client portals, and market connectivity?
What are the SLAs for remediating critical and high penetration test findings?
Are penetration testers independent and appropriately qualified?
How do penetration testing findings compare year-over-year - are there recurring issues?
redFlags: No penetration testing conducted in the last 12 months
Testing scope excludes critical trading systems or client-facing applications
Critical findings from penetration testing remain unresolved beyond SLA

maturityIndicators.basic: No regular penetration testing; scope limited; findings not tracked
maturityIndicators.mature: Annual testing with qualified independent testers, comprehensive scope, SLA-driven remediation, and audit committee reporting
maturityIndicators.advanced: Continuous red team exercises with purple team collaboration, automated vulnerability validation, and year-round testing cadence

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that a governance framework exists for algorithmic trading systems with approval processes, testing requirements, and ongoing monitoring.

testSteps.action: Obtain the algorithmic trading governance framework or policy and verify it defines the approval process for new algorithms, testing requirements, and periodic review obligations.
Select 3 algorithms deployed in the last 12 months and review the approval records, including risk assessment, compliance sign-off, and authorisation before production deployment.
Review testing records for the selected algorithms, including backtesting results, simulation testing in non-production environments, and stress testing under extreme market conditions.
Inspect production monitoring dashboards or reports for algorithm performance and risk metrics, including evidence that anomalies are detected and investigated.
Review records of periodic algorithm reviews and re-approvals, verifying that algorithms are reviewed at least annually for continued appropriateness.
Interview the Head of Trading and Compliance to understand how governance processes interact and who has authority to suspend an algorithm in production.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: Governance framework; approval records for 3 recently deployed algorithms; testing records; monitoring reports; periodic review records
keyQuestions: What is the end-to-end approval process for deploying a new algorithm to production?
What testing is required before an algorithm can be deployed (backtesting, simulation, stress testing)?
How are algorithms monitored in production and what triggers a review or suspension?
How often are existing algorithms reviewed and re-approved?
redFlags: Algorithms deployed to production without formal approval or compliance sign-off
No backtesting or simulation testing conducted before production deployment
No periodic review of existing algorithms; algorithms running indefinitely without reassessment

maturityIndicators.basic: No formal algorithm governance; algorithms deployed without approval process or testing requirements
maturityIndicators.mature: Documented approval and testing process with compliance involvement, production monitoring, and annual review
maturityIndicators.advanced: Automated algorithm monitoring with anomaly detection, continuous performance tracking, and dynamic risk-based review triggers

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that risks from AI/ML use in capital market operations are assessed and managed, including model risk, bias, and explainability requirements.

testSteps.action: Obtain an inventory of AI/ML applications used in capital market operations (trading, compliance screening, client onboarding, risk management) and verify each has been risk-assessed.

Review model risk assessment documentation for the highest-risk AI/ML applications, verifying that model risk is assessed across dimensions of accuracy, bias, explainability, and operational dependency.
Inspect model validation and testing procedures, including evidence that models are validated before production deployment and periodically thereafter.
Review bias assessment records for client-facing AI/ML applications and verify that mitigation actions are documented and implemented where bias is identified.
Verify that explainability requirements are defined for AI/ML-driven decisions that affect clients (e.g., credit scoring, investment recommendations, KYC screening) and that outputs can be explained.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: AI/ML application inventory; model risk assessments for highest-risk applications; validation records; bias assessments for client-facing models
keyQuestions: What AI/ML applications are used in trading, compliance, or client-facing operations?
How are AI/ML model risks assessed and who is accountable for model risk?
What bias testing is conducted for AI/ML models that affect client outcomes?
Can the entity explain AI/ML-driven decisions to clients and regulators if required?
redFlags: No inventory of AI/ML applications or no risk assessment conducted
AI/ML models deployed in trading or compliance without model validation
No bias assessment for client-facing AI/ML applications

maturityIndicators.basic: No AI/ML risk framework; models deployed without risk assessment or validation
maturityIndicators.mature: Model risk management for AI applications with validation, bias assessment, and explainability requirements
maturityIndicators.advanced: Comprehensive AI governance with continuous monitoring, automated drift detection, and real-time explainability

testingProcedures.objective: Verify that risk management controls are implemented for DLT and digital asset activities, including smart contract security, key management, and regulatory compliance.

testSteps.action: Determine whether the entity engages in DLT or digital asset activities; if so, obtain an inventory of all DLT/digital asset deployments and their risk assessments.
Review smart contract security assessments conducted before deployment, including independent code audits and formal verification where applicable.
Inspect key management and wallet controls for digital assets, including generation, storage (HSM, cold storage), access controls, and recovery procedures.
Verify regulatory compliance for digital asset activities under SC guidelines, including licensing status, custody requirements, and investor protection measures.
Review DLT infrastructure monitoring arrangements, including node health, consensus mechanism monitoring, and security incident detection.

testingProcedures.sampleSize: DLT/digital asset inventory; smart contract audit reports; key management documentation; SC regulatory compliance records; infrastructure monitoring evidence
keyQuestions: What DLT or digital asset activities does the entity conduct, if any?
Have all deployed smart contracts been subject to independent security audit?
How are private keys and digital asset wallets secured, and what recovery procedures exist?
Is the entity's digital asset activity compliant with SC licensing and custody requirements?
redFlags: Smart contracts deployed without independent security assessment
Private keys stored in software wallets without HSM protection or adequate access controls
Digital asset activities conducted without appropriate SC licensing or regulatory compliance assessment

maturityIndicators.basic: No DLT-specific controls; digital asset activities without formal risk management
maturityIndicators.mature: Security assessments for smart contracts, HSM-based key management, and documented regulatory compliance
maturityIndicators.advanced: Formal verification of smart contracts, continuous DLT monitoring, multi-party computation for key management, and proactive regulatory engagement
